claim: OpenShell enforces security policies out-of-process (supervisor enforces before requests reach real systems) rather than in-process (prompts/rules inside agent); enables safety even if agent is compromised
actionable: Use out-of-process enforcement (supervisor layer) for agent security; don't rely on agent to enforce its own rules via system prompts

claim: OpenShell supervisor controls four attack surfaces: network (default-deny allowlist), files (sandbox isolation, no host directory access), inference (managed endpoints), credentials (injected at runtime, never on disk)
actionable: Implement four-layer sandbox: deny-by-default network policies, isolated file system, managed inference routing, runtime credential injection

claim: NemoClaw blueprints (harness + model + runtime) are modular; same runtime (OpenShell) works with different harnesses (OpenClaw, Hermes, LangChain DeepAgents) and models
actionable: Build agents using modular blueprint pattern: separate harness (planning logic), model (reasoning), and runtime (execution sandbox); swap harness/model independently

claim: Default-deny network policies (with explicit allowlist) enable compliance teams to verify agent won't exfiltrate data; policy files are code and can be reviewed/diffed
actionable: Document agent security posture as policy files (YAML); use policy-as-code to enable compliance review without black-box trust

claim: Network and inference policies can hot-reload in OpenShell, but file system policies cannot; design with immutable file permissions to prevent privilege escalation
actionable: Plan file system policies carefully in OpenShell; file access is locked at sandbox creation and can't be changed without recreating sandbox

claim: For production agents, think in terms of harness + sandbox + security primitives rather than pure frameworks; runtime layer (execution environment) is as important as agent logic
actionable: When deploying agents to production, prioritize security runtime (sandbox, policy enforcement) as much as agent harness and model selection
